strategy_research: {"strategies": [{"name": "Dual Momentum (GEM) \u2014 Antonacci", "description": "Each month-end: if SPY 12-month return beats T-bills, hold whichever of SPY or EFA has higher 12-month return; otherwise hold AGG. Pure absolute + relative momentum in three ETFs. Single holding at all times.", "evidence": ["Antonacci, G. (2012) 'Risk Premia Harvesting Through Dual Momentum,' SSRN", "Geczy & Samonov (2015) '215-Year Evidence on Momentum,' confirms momentum back to 1801", "Antonacci (2019) 'Extended Backtest of Global Equities Momentum,' Medium \u2014 index-based 1974\u20132013"], "historical_sharpe": "0.85\u20131.0 (index-based 1974\u20132013); ~0.50\u20130.65 on ETFs live post-2013 due to US equity dominance and 2022 bond collapse", "market_conditions": "Works: sustained bear markets (2000\u20132002, 2008\u20132009), trending international divergence. Fails: prolonged US bull markets, simultaneous equity+bond crashes (2022), V-shaped recoveries (COVID April 2020 whipsaw)", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Vigilant Asset Allocation (VAA-G4) \u2014 Keller & Keuning", "description": "Monthly momentum score = 12\u00d7(p0/p1) + 4\u00d7(p0/p3) + 2\u00d7(p0/p6) + (p0/p12) \u2212 19 for offensive universe (SPY, EFA, EEM, AGG). If any offensive asset has negative score, rotate to top defensive asset (SHY/IEF/BIL). Otherwise hold top-ranked offensive asset 100%.", "evidence": ["Keller, W.J. & Keuning, J.W. (2017) 'Breadth Momentum and Vigilant Asset Allocation,' SSRN 3002624", "Keller (2018) 'Defensive Asset Allocation (DAA),' SSRN \u2014 follow-up addressing VAA's excess cash problem", "AllocateSmartly independent live tracking (2017\u2013present)"], "historical_sharpe": "1.10 in-sample (backtest); post-publication live performance deteriorated to ~0.65\u20130.75 as excess defensive cash allocation suppressed returns in 2019\u20132021 bull market", "market_conditions": "Works: volatile bear markets, trending regimes with clear breadth deterioration. Fails: prolonged low-vol bull markets (excess cash drag), high-turnover environments, potential overfitting to historical data (AGG inclusion flags)", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG", "TLT"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Trend-Following Multi-Timeframe (TSMOM) with Vol Targeting", "description": "Rank SPY, QQQ, IWM, GLD, TLT, EFA by composite momentum score across 1-month, 3-month, 6-month, and 12-month lookbacks (equal-weighted). Long top 2\u20133 assets with positive trend across majority of timeframes. Scale each position to target 10% annualized volatility. Monthly rebalance.", "evidence": ["Moskowitz, Ooi & Pedersen (2012) 'Time Series Momentum,' Journal of Financial Economics 104(2)", "Hurst, Ooi & Pedersen (2017) 'A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following,' Journal of Portfolio Management 44(1) \u2014 AQR, 1880\u20132016 across 67 markets", "Barroso & Santa-Clara (2015) 'Momentum Has Its Moments,' JFE \u2014 volatility targeting doubles Sharpe", "arXiv (2025) 'Follow the Leader: Network Momentum' \u2014 cross-asset spillover signals improve Sharpe further"], "historical_sharpe": "1.2\u20131.4 gross (diversified futures, multi-decade); 0.88\u20131.05 for ETF-based multi-timeframe implementations; vol-scaling typically doubles base Sharpe vs. unscaled version", "market_conditions": "Works: persistent macro trends, commodity supercycles, sustained equity bull/bear markets, crisis periods (positive in 8 of 10 largest 60/40 drawdowns since 1880). Fails: range-bound choppy markets, V-shaped reversals, QE-suppressed volatility 2010\u20132019", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "GLD", "TLT", "EFA"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Risk Parity with Volatility Targeting (Equal Risk Contribution)", "description": "Size positions in SPY, TLT, GLD, and EFA so each contributes equal risk (not equal dollar weight) to the portfolio. Use 20-day rolling volatility for position sizing. Target 8\u201310% portfolio volatility. Rebalance monthly. No leverage required at ETF level \u2014 just underweight equities and overweight bonds/gold relative to naive 60/40.", "evidence": ["Qian, E. (2005) PanAgora white paper coining 'risk parity'", "Asness, Frazzini & Pedersen (2012) 'Leverage Aversion and Risk Parity,' Financial Analysts Journal 68(1)", "Hurst, Johnson & Ooi (2010) 'Understanding Risk Parity,' AQR white paper", "AQR backtest (1947\u20132015): RP Sharpe 0.75 vs. 60/40 Sharpe 0.52"], "historical_sharpe": "0.75\u20130.85 gross (AQR, 1947\u20132015 at 10% vol target); 60/40 benchmark ~0.52 same period; Bridgewater All Weather ~0.60 multi-decade", "market_conditions": "Works: negative or zero stock-bond correlation regimes (1980\u20132021), macroeconomic uncertainty, diversified multi-regime environments. Fails: positive stock-bond correlation (2022 was catastrophic \u2014 simultaneous equity+bond decline), rising rate shocks, 1990s equity-only bull", "instruments": ["SPY", "TLT", "GLD", "EFA", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Adaptive Asset Rotation \u2014 Stacked Signal Ensemble", "description": "For each instrument (SPY, QQQ, IWM, GLD, TLT, EFA), compute three uncorrelated signals: (1) 12-1 month absolute momentum, (2) 20-day volatility-adjusted momentum (return/vol), (3) 50/200 EMA crossover trend filter. Equal-weight the three normalized signals into a composite score. Long top 3 assets with positive composite; cash if composite negative. Rebalance monthly.", "evidence": ["Goldman Sachs AM (2018) 'How to Combine Investment Signals in Long/Short' \u2014 mixed signal approach Sharpe 0.68 vs. 0.59 integrated", "Ehsani & Linnainmaa (2019) 'Factor Momentum and the Momentum Factor,' NBER w25551 \u2014 combining 20 factors raises Sharpe materially", "J.P. Morgan (2015) 'Momentum Strategies Across Asset Classes' \u2014 equal-marginal-vol portfolio Sharpe 0.78 (1972\u20132014)", "ScienceDirect (2025) Characteristic-Managed Momentum: Sharpe 1.47 vs. 0.49 standard momentum via learned signal weighting"], "historical_sharpe": "0.95\u20131.25 depending on signal combination; diversification across uncorrelated signals (momentum + trend + vol-adjusted) typically adds 0.3\u20130.5 Sharpe vs. single-signal; best implementations reach 1.47", "market_conditions": "Works: when individual signals are genuinely uncorrelated (momentum + trend confirm together), multi-regime environments. Fails: crisis signal correlation (all signals simultaneously defensive), data mining bias inflates IS Sharpe, high turnover costs", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "GLD", "TLT", "EFA", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Regime-Based Rotation \u2014 VIX + Yield Curve Filter", "description": "Rule-based macro regime overlay on top of a base momentum strategy. Regime rules: (1) If VIX < 20 AND 10Y-2Y yield spread > 0 \u2192 full risk-on (100% SPY/QQQ). (2) If VIX 20\u201330 OR yield curve flat \u2192 balanced allocation (SPY + TLT + GLD equal weight). (3) If VIX > 30 OR yield curve inverted \u2192 risk-off (TLT + GLD + AGG). Weekly regime check, monthly rebalance.", "evidence": ["Faust, Gilchrist, Wright & Zakrajsek (2012) 'Credit Spreads as Predictors of Real-Time Economic Activity,' NBER \u2014 credit spreads + yield curve best real-time recession predictors", "Scientific Reports (2025) ML framework VIX + credit spreads OOS Sharpe 1.38 (2017\u20132022)", "Dynamic Factor Allocation Using Regime-Switching Signals, arXiv 2410.14841 (2024)", "VIX-filtered strategies: +55% vs. traditional risk parity across multi-period studies"], "historical_sharpe": "1.05\u20131.38 in recent OOS studies (2017\u20132022); rule-based implementations more modest at ~0.85\u20131.05; strong in 2022 (unlike pure momentum or risk parity)", "market_conditions": "Works: clear macro regime transitions (2000\u20132002, 2008\u20132009, 2022 rate shock), prolonged bear markets with rising VIX. Fails: VIX spikes that quickly reverse (2018 Volmageddon), inverted curve without recession (1998), fast central bank interventions that suppress signals", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "TLT", "GLD", "AGG"], "timeframe": "weekly signal check, monthly rebalance", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Hierarchical Risk Parity (HRP) \u2014 Lopez de Prado", "description": "Build a correlation-based dendrogram of all instruments. Reorder the covariance matrix via quasi-diagonalization. Allocate weights top-down via recursive bisection using inverse-variance at each tree node. No matrix inversion required. Result: diversified allocation that respects asset cluster structure. Rebalance monthly using 60-day rolling covariance.", "evidence": ["Lopez de Prado, M. (2016) 'Building Diversified Portfolios that Outperform Out-of-Sample,' Journal of Portfolio Management 42(4), SSRN 2708678", "ScienceDirect (2025) 'Hierarchical Risk Parity: Efficient Implementation and Real World Analysis' \u2014 turnover-adjusted comparison vs. ERC and 1/N", "QuantPedia live tracking and replication studies"], "historical_sharpe": "~0.94\u20131.0 net after transaction costs (turnover ~18%/month); gross advantage over ERC (~1.0 vs 1.26) disappears net of costs on small universes; strongest advantage in large heterogeneous universes (20+ assets)", "market_conditions": "Works: large heterogeneous asset universes, ill-conditioned covariance matrices, compared to mean-variance optimization (avoids concentration). Fails: small 8-asset universes (1/N is competitive), high-turnover environments, rapid correlation regime shifts (2020, 2022)", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "VTI", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG", "EFA"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "high"}, {"name": "Defensive Asset Allocation (DAA) \u2014 Keller (Enhanced VAA)", "description": "Improvement over VAA using a separate two-asset 'canary universe' (VWO + BND) for crash detection, separate from the offensive trading universe. If both canaries have negative momentum \u2192 100% cash. If one canary negative \u2192 50% cash + 50% top offensive asset. If both canaries positive \u2192 100% top offensive asset from (SPY, EFA, GLD, TLT). Reduces average defensive allocation from VAA's ~60% to <30%.", "evidence": ["Keller, W.J. (2018) 'Breadth Momentum and the Canary Universe: Defensive Asset Allocation (DAA),' SSRN", "AllocateSmartly independent replication and live tracking (2018\u2013present)", "Addresses VAA's documented weakness of excess cash allocation during bull markets"], "historical_sharpe": "1.10\u20131.20 backtest; addresses VAA's post-publication bull-market drag; more efficient cash deployment than VAA while retaining crash protection; live tracking modestly below backtest but better than raw VAA post-2018", "market_conditions": "Works: crash detection via canary universe provides earlier/cleaner signal than VAA's breadth rule; better bull-market participation. Fails: same overfitting concerns as VAA; canary universe (VWO+BND) may diverge from actual offensive universe stress in novel regimes", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "GLD", "TLT", "AGG"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}], "recommendation": "Start with the Trend-Following Multi-Timeframe strategy (TSMOM with vol targeting) because it has the deepest academic evidence base (Moskowitz/AQR 1880\u20132016 across 67 markets), is naturally suited to the preferred 'trend_following_multi_timeframe' strategy family, and the vol-targeting overlay is proven to double the base Sharpe ratio. It uses all 6 preferred instruments (SPY, QQQ, IWM, GLD, TLT, EFA), requires only monthly rebalancing, and is implementable with $1,000. The composite momentum score across 1/3/6/12-month lookbacks avoids single-horizon brittleness \u2014 the empirical Sharpe of 0.88\u20131.05 for ETF implementations is achievable without leverage. As a second layer, add the Regime-Based VIX+Yield Curve filter as an overlay: when VIX > 30 or yield curve is inverted, reduce equity exposure to GLD+TLT. This hybrid captures trend-following alpha in normal regimes while providing macro-aware crash protection that pure momentum lacks (critical lesson from 2022). The Dual Momentum GEM is the simplest fallback and well-validated, but its ETF-era live performance has been weaker than its index backtest suggests, and it lacks the multi-instrument diversification of TSMOM."}
